coredata.dc:title: A comprehensive review on nanotechnology based sensors for monitoring quality and shelf life of food products
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Ghosh, Tabli
Raj, G.V.S. Bhagya
Dash, Kshirod Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Nanosensors are chemical or mechanical sensors used in food and water quality detection to detect the presence of chemical species. Biosensor enabled packaging systems can detect food quality and extend shelf life. Nanosensors provide faster and more accurate detection of microbes, toxins, and adulterants. Nanoparticles may also be used to detect biodegradable food components such as vitamins and antioxidants. The review focuses on the emerging application of nanotechnology based sensors for shelf life analysis of food products, including freshness monitoring, food safety measurement, and detection of spoiled food components. The focused categories of nanomaterials for developing sensors in food applications are inorganic, organic, and carbon allotropes. In this context, the characteristics features of inorganic nanomaterials, organic nanomaterials, and carbon allotropes for developing nanotechnology-based sensors have been discussed. Further, a detailed discussion on the use of inorganic nanomaterials such as gold, silver, titanium dioxide, and zinc oxide nanoparticles for developing sensors in determining food shelf life (freshness, spoilage, and food safety) has been made. Due to available significant properties, including biocompatibility, nontoxicity, photochemical activity, large surface area, and electronic properties, the inorganic nanomaterials are a promising candidate for developing sensors to be used in food sectors. Besides, the inclusion of organic or biopolymeric nanomaterials and carbon allotropes for developing sensors for food shelf life has also been discussed. This study revealed that nanosensors have a substantial role in the food industry as identification and detection tool.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanotechnology
Shelf-life
Inorganic nanomaterials
Organic nanomaterials
Carbon allotropes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Targeted nanomaterials for fabricating sensors in shelf life detection of food products
Inorganic nanomaterials for developing sensors in shelf life detection
Application of nanotechnology in freshness and shelf life detection of food products
Application of titanium dioxide based sensors
Application of TiO₂ based sensor to detect synthetic colorants in food
Application of silver nanoparticles based sensors
Application of gold nanoparticles based sensors
Application of various nanoparticles based sensors

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Targeted Materials in Nanotechnology based Sensors for Shelf life of Food Products and Safety Analysis.

row.entry:
Titanium dioxide
Gold nanoparticles
Silver nanoparticles
Zinc oxide
Quantum dots
Magnetic nanoparticles
Others

Nanostructured films
Nanostructured hydrogel
Dendrimer
Hyperbranched polymeric nanoparticles
Molecularly imprinted polymeric nanoparticles
Polymer nanocomposites

Graphene
Reduced graphene oxide
Carbon nanotube
Fullerenes
Carbon dots
Others

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Titanium dioxide based sensor in shelf life monitoring and food safety

row.entry: Graphene and mesoporous TiO₂
Sunset yellow and tartrazine
Real food samples such as royal jelly, candy, ice cream, soft drink, juice powder, and others

Titanium dioxide-polyaniline (TiO₂-PANI)
Silk fibroin microfiber (SFF)
Total volatile basic nitrogen level

list-item.para: 86.38% of discriminating accuracy for pork freshness

row.entry: Pork freshness

Polypyrrole/ TiO₂
based gas sensor coated on Quartz crystal microbalance substrate
Trace irritant gases such as NH3, ethanol, H2S, trimethylamine

list-item.para: Sensitive to toxic gases at low concentrations

row.entry: Evaluate the shelf life of mango, egg and fish
Schiff base Zn(II) complex/ TiO₂ as Histamine fluorescence sensor
Detect histamine
Salmon fillet

SnO₂ thick film based sensor with TiO₂ and transition metals
[Sensor materials: Pd (1 wt%), TiO₂ (10 wt%) and SnO₂]
Gas sensor

list-item.para: Good selectivity for dimethylamine compared to TMA gas
Good selectivity for ammonia compared to TMA gas

row.entry: Fish freshness
TiO₂-Ag/Polypyrrole nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode
Electrochemical sensor
Food samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Silver Nanoparticle based sensor in shelf life monitoring and food safety

row.entry: Electrochemical sensor based on Carbamazepine functionalized AgNPs

Detect specific toxic dyes such as
orange II and rhodamine B

list-item.para: Detection limit for Rhodamine B is 1.2 nM
Detection limit for orange II is 1.0 Nm

row.entry: Surface enhanced Raman scattering sensor based on graphene and silver nanocomposite
Detect prohibited food colorants

list-item.para: Detection limit is as low as 0.1 µM

row.entry: Electrochemical sensor based on AgNPs and carboxylic short-chain multi-wall carbon nanotubes
Detect chloramphenicol in food
Gellan gum and AgNPs based colorimetric hydrogen sulphide sensor
Monitor meat spoilage in intelligent packaging
L-cysteine modified AgNPs
Detect Vitamin B1 in food and water samples

list-item.para: Selective detection of vitamin B1 in food such as grapes, peas, tomato
Selective detection of vitamin B1 in environmental water samples such as sewage, river, and pond.
Limit of detection is 7.0 µg mL−1

row.entry: Graphene quantum dots and cysteine capped AgNPs
Detect glyphosate in food samples

list-item.para: Limit of detection is 9 ng mL−1
Quantification of glyphosate in pulses (peas and lupins)

row.entry: AgNPs and graphene oxide nanocomposite based electrochemical sensor
Detect sodium ions

list-item.para: Detection of sodium ions in fish sauce, and seasoning powder of instant noodle.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Gold nanoparticle based sensor in shelf life monitoring and food safety

row.entry: Chemically modified Carbon paste electrode with gold nanoparticles
Sunset yellow and tartrazine
Routine analysis of the dyes in Soft drinks
Multiwalled carbon nanotube and gold NPs composites and chitosan
Detection of tyramine
Tyramine detection in yoghurt samples
Enhanced chemiluminescence immunosensor based on AuNPs
Detection of Staphylococcal Enterotoxin B in food
Detection of Staphylococcal Enterotoxin B in mushroom, tomato and meat baby foods
Colorimetric aptasensor based on AuNPs and aptamer
Detection of Shigella flexneri in food samples

list-item.para: Naked eye detection of Shigella flexneri

row.entry: Smoked salmon samples
Voltammetric sensor based on AuNPs
Determination of Sudan I

Food samples such as ketchup and chilly sauce
(Spiked with sudan I)
Surface imprinted AuNPs based surface enhanced Raman Scattering based sensor
Detection of patulin
Food samples
Colorimetric sensor based on AuNPs
Detection of reducing sugars

list-item.para: Can determine different sugars such as glucose, fructose, mannose and lactose

row.entry: Food samples such as beverages, and milk

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Zinc Nanoparticle based sensor in shelf life monitoring and food safety

row.entry: Smell sensor based on aluminium doped zinc oxide thin film
Detect odour of seafoods Salmon, oyster, squid, sea bream and sardine

list-item.para: High selectivity for Trimethylamine gas present in odour

row.entry: Thick film sensor based on tin oxide and zinc oxide
Vapour sensing properties for electrical response to dimethyldisulphide and 1-butanol

list-item.para: Can monitor quality of potato tubers and dry cured ham in storage

row.entry: Fluorescence optical sensor based on zinc(II) salphen complex
Biogenic amine detection

list-item.para: Good selectivity for histamine and cadaverine
Limit of detection 4.4 × 10−12 M for histamine

row.entry: Electrochemical sensor based on zinc oxide nanoflower modified carbon paste electrode
Trace detection of sunset yellow in soft drinks

head.ce:title: A comprehensive review on nanotechnology based sensors for monitoring quality and shelf life of food products

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Processing Technology, Ghani Khan Choudhury Institute of Engineering and Technology (GKCIET), Malda, West Bengal, 732141, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ghani Khan Choudhury Institute of Engineering and Technology (GKCIET)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Food Processing Technology, Ghani Khan Choudhury Institute of Engineering and Technology (GKCIET), Malda, West Bengal, 732141, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Engineering and Technology, Tezpur University, Tezpur, Assam, 784028, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Food Engineering and Technology, Tezpur University, Tezpur, Assam, 784028, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanosensors are chemical or mechanical sensors used in food and water quality detection to detect the presence of chemical species. Biosensor enabled packaging systems can detect food quality and extend shelf life. Nanosensors provide faster and more accurate detection of microbes, toxins, and adulterants. Nanoparticles may also be used to detect biodegradable food components such as vitamins and antioxidants. The review focuses on the emerging application of nanotechnology based sensors for shelf life analysis of food products, including freshness monitoring, food safety measurement, and detection of spoiled food components. The focused categories of nanomaterials for developing sensors in food applications are inorganic, organic, and carbon allotropes. In this context, the characteristics features of inorganic nanomaterials, organic nanomaterials, and carbon allotropes for developing nanotechnology-based sensors have been discussed. Further, a detailed discussion on the use of inorganic nanomaterials such as gold, silver, titanium dioxide, and zinc oxide nanoparticles for developing sensors in determining food shelf life (freshness, spoilage, and food safety) has been made. Due to available significant properties, including biocompatibility, nontoxicity, photochemical activity, large surface area, and electronic properties, the inorganic nanomaterials are a promising candidate for developing sensors to be used in food sectors. Besides, the inclusion of organic or biopolymeric nanomaterials and carbon allotropes for developing sensors for food shelf life has also been discussed. This study revealed that nanosensors have a substantial role in the food industry as identification and detection tool.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials are extensively used for several food applications such as sensors for food analysis, biodegradable packaging, edible food packaging, intelligent packaging, and active packaging. Additionally, nanomaterials are used in food sectors as nano additives, nanocapsules, gelating agents, nanocarriers, anticaking agents, etc. The main function of nanotechnology in food security includes food safety, preservation, and functionalization [1]. Additionally, food quality and safety need to be measured for the safe delivery of food products to end-users. In this context, several analytical techniques are extensively used to measure the stability of the food components, including spectrometric or chromatographic. However, the mentioned processes are time-consuming and laborious, which directs the development of sensors for detecting the food components (vitamins), ethylene concentrations, volatile gas, biogenic amines, and toxins. The detection of these components can evaluate the food quality, as food produces different kinds of volatile gases, amines, and others if quality degradation occurs. In this regard, several amines, such as trimethylamine, ammonia, and dimethylamine are considered as indicators of fish freshness [2].

Additionally, the shelf life analysis of food products is very crucial as it includes the measures of food quality (freshness and quality degrading components) and safety. However, the shelf life analysis of food products during storage generally includes the detection of nutritional value compared to fresh food products, the occurrence of any toxic component (patulin, and Staphylococcal Enterotoxin B), presence of undesirable food additives (Sunset yellow and tartrazine, orange II and rhodamine B), and others. The undesirable food quality may develop during the whole storage life, starting from raw materials collection, processing effects or storage time, and after transportation. Some external components such as diverse toxins, undesirable additives (such as colorants), drugs, and pesticides may also reduce the shelf life or create problems during the whole life cycle of food products. In this context, several kinds of the sensor are developed to analyze the food freshness, spoiled food products, and safety measures.

In food sectors, sensors are used for detecting pathogens, adulterants, degradation metabolites, toxins, and allergens. Additionally, freshness sensors in food include direct (freshness, ripeness, microbial, leakage) or indirect sensors (time-temperature indicator, radio frequency identification). Additionally, biosensors are based on transducing elements (electrochemical biosensor, optical biosensor, and mass-based biosensor) and bio recognition elements (antibody, DNA, enzyme).

In this context, the current review demonstrates the several targeted nanomaterials, including inorganic nanomaterials, organic/polymeric nanomaterials and carbon allotropes used in developing sensors for shelf life of food products. The mentioned nanomaterials in conjugation with other materials are focused on developing sensors with unique properties such as sensitivity, selectivity, catalytic activity, and chemical properties. Additionally, the review uses several kinds of sensors, including gas, electrochemical, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, chemiluminescence immunosensor, colorimetric aptasensor, voltammetric sensor, colorimetric sensor, optical fluorescence sensor, edible pH sensor, etc. in determining food shelf life.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeted nanomaterials for fabricating sensors in shelf life detection of food products
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Table 1 , the various targeted nanomaterials in developing sensors include organic, inorganic, and carbon-allotrope based materials. In this context, the various characteristics features of available nanomaterials have been discussed in the below sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inorganic nanomaterials for developing sensors in shelf life detection
ce:section.ce:para: The various inorganic nanomaterials for developing sensors in shelf life detection and quality monitoring of food products include titanium dioxide, gold nanoparticles, silver nanoparticles, zinc oxide, quantum dots, and magnetic nanoparticles.

Titanium oxide is a transition metal oxide with the phase structures of anatase, rutile, and brookite. It is an inexpensive, non-toxic, inert metal with potential action against various microorganisms [3]. The anatase and rutile have a tetragonal structure, and brookite has an orthorhombic structure. The nanoform of TiO2 has the properties of biocompatibility, nontoxicity, cost-effectiveness, high UV absorbance, high photochemical activity, large surface area along with remarkable chemical and electronic properties. The TiO2 based sensors are used in sensing volatile chemicals and detecting soluble organics and biological substances. In this context, this inorganic nanomaterial is extensively used in developing gas sensors, chemical oxygen demand sensors, and biosensors for biological substance detection. Additionally, TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) are extensively used in various sectors such as agriculture, sericulture, gas sensing, medical devices, food industry, cosmetic industry, solar cells, paper industry, plastic industry, and waste water treatment process [4]. Chi et al., (2019) reported that nano composite film formed by polylactic acid (PLA), bergamot essential oils, and titanium oxide (TiO2) efficiently stored the mangoes for 15 days at room temperature by delaying the changes in quality characteristics such as color, firmness, vitamin C and also restricted the microbial activity when correlated with PLA based film [5].

Besides as a biosensor, titanium dioxide is widely used as sensor in several ways, such as (i) Detection of pork freshness [6], (ii) Evaluate the shelf life of mango, egg, and fish [7], (iii) Detection of histamine in Salmon fillet [8], (iv) As gas sensor in determining fish freshness [9].

The synthesis of AgNPs is done following various methods such as (i) Physical methods: Evaporation/condensation, Laser ablation, thermal decomposition; (ii) Chemical methods: Chemical reduction method, photochemical method, electrochemical method, sonochemical methods; and (iii) Biological methods: Bacteria mediated, fungi mediated, algae mediated, plant mediated synthesis. The silver nanoparticles have the properties of antiviral, antibacterials, anti-inflammatory, anti-angiogenic, antitumor, and antioxidative. The antibacterial application of silver NPs includes footwear, apparel, paints, plastics, and appliances. However, the major sectors of AgNPs include textiles (UV blocking textile, anti-stain textile, medical textiles), environmental (water disinfection, wastewater treatment, activated carbon filters), catalysis (fuel cell catalysts, fuel additive catalyst, hydrogen production photocatalysts), food agriculture (packaging, interactive food, quality analysis and biosensors), health care (UV protection, creams, nutraceuticals), etc. [10]. Ma et al. (2011) detected melamine in raw milk with silver nanoparticles and dopamine sensor [11]. Traiwatcharanon et al. (2020) developed an electrochemical sensor by the accumulation of silver nanoparticles and graphene oxide on the screen-printed silver electrode to detect the sodium ion present in fish sauce and seasoning powder of instant noodles [53]. The sensor reported to detect the sodium ions by oxidation/reduction reactions of the nanoparticles and has high sensitivity and selectivity and the ability to detect small amounts with decent stability.

Furthermore, AgNPs based sensors are extensively used, such as (i) Detection of specific toxic dyes [12] (ii) Prohibited food colorants [13] (iii) Detection of chloramphenicol in food [14] (iv) Monitor meat spoilage in intelligent packaging [15] (v) Detection of Vitamin B1 in food and water samples [16], (vi) Detection of glyphosate in food samples [17].

The properties of gold nanoparticles include chemically inert, biological compatibility, high stability, high surface area to volume ratio, high dispersity, non-cytotoxicity, plasmonic nanoparticles, strong scattering properties, and high electrical and heat conductivity [18]. The sensor based on AuNPs depends on the color change of the solution or aggregation/disaggregation of particles. These NPs are used in detecting hazardous agents, toxins, pesticide drugs, and heavy metals. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are extensively used in several ways, such as drug delivery, radiotherapy, photothermal therapy, imaging agent, nanoenzymes, cancer therapy, biosensor, tissue engineering, and enzyme immobilization. Bhardwaj et al. (2020) successfully detected known concentrations of Aflatoxin B1 (0.1 nM and 1 nM) from contaminated wheat flour by surface plasmon resonance biosensor fabricated with gold nanoparticles that acted as signal amplifiers in detecting the small targeted molecule with higher recovery rate (>90.1%) [19]. AuNPs based sensors are also used in detecting sunset yellow and tartrazine in soft drinks [20]; detecting tyramine in yogurt [21]; detecting Staphylococcal Enterotoxin B in mushroom and tomato [22]; detecting Shigella flexneri in smoked salmon [23]; determining Sudan I in hot chilly powder [24]; detecting patulin in blueberry sauce, grapefruit sauce, and orange juice [25]; detecting reducing sugars in lactose free milk and beverages [26], etc.

The zinc oxide NPs generally have diameters less than 100 nm, whereas, the NPs have high catalytic activity and high surface area. The several synthesis processes include hydrothermal methods, laser ablation, electrochemical depositions, chemical vapor deposition, sol-gel method, thermal decomposition, anodization, co-precipitation, ultrasound, etc. Further, the plant mediated synthesis of ZnO NPs includes a simple process, environmentally friendly, inexpensive, biocompatible reagents, and no additional chemicals. Das & Sarkar (2017) developed polyaniline incorporated ZnO nanocomposite for the detection of NH3 and reported that the sensor detected ammonia gas with a quick response time and better stability at room temperature [27]. The biomedical application of ZnO NPs includes tissue engineering, anticancer, antibacterial, angiogenesis, drug delivery, immune therapy, gene delivery, biosensing, bioimaging, antidiabetic, etc. [28]. Further, ZnO NPs are extensively used in developing a sensor for sensing food properties during storage. Zinc oxide and its nanoforms are used in detecting odors of seafood [29], vapor sensing properties for monitoring quality during storage [30], biogenic amine detection [31], and trace detection of sunset yellow in soft drinks [32].

The quantum dots are artificial nanocrystals that can transport electrons. The quantum dots are fabricated following colloidal synthesis, plasma synthesis, viral assembly, electrochemical assembly, etc. The quantum dots are used in photovoltaic devices (solar cells, hybrid solar cells), light emitting diodes, photocatalysts, photodetector devices, etc. Durán et al. (2015) developed a β-cyclo dextrin modified Cd Se/ZnS QDs sensor to determine vanillin in milk and sugar [33]. Additionally, graphene quantum dots are used as sensors for detecting phenols in olive oil [34], as an optical sensor for glucose [35], photoluminescent sensor for pesticide detection [36], visual detection of copper ions [37], and further used as multifunctional sensors. However, quantum dots have wide usability in the field of drug delivery, imaging, and sensor applications [38].

The class of magnetic nanoparticles can be controlled using magnetic fields. This kind of NPs contains two components such as magnetic materials (iron, cobalt, nickel) and chemical component. The several types of magnetic NPs are oxides, ferrites with a shell, metallic, and metallic with a shell. The synthesis of magnetic NPs includes co-precipitation, thermal decomposition, microemulsion, flame spray synthesis, etc. Magnetic nanoparticles are widely used in several sectors such as medical diagnostics, catalysis, magnetic immunoassay, molecular imaging, waste water treatment, cancer therapy, biosensor, bioseparation, information storage, etc. The electrochemical biosensors based on magnetic nanomaterials are used in the detection of synthetic food colorants, antioxidants, heavy metal ions, antibiotics, and others [39]. Bharath et al. (2018) successfully detected caffeic acid in coffee by fabricating Au@α-Fe2O3@RGO electrochemical sensor [40]. Similarly, many researchers developed sensors for the determination of quercetin in tea [41], dimethoate in Chinese cabbage [42], kanamycin in pork meat [43], and aflatoxin M1 in milk [44].

The organic nanomaterials such as polymeric nanomaterials in the form of nanostructured films, nanostructured hydrogel, dendrimer, hyperbranched polymeric nanoparticles, covalent organic frameworks, molecularly imprinted polymeric nanoparticles, polymer nanocomposites, etc. are used in developing sensors. The different conducting polymers such as polyaniline and polypyrole, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) are used to develop sensors. Further, the nanoforms of cellulose, chitosan, and other biopolymers are used in combination with others in developing sensors [45,46].

Carbon can form different structures of the same element because of its valency. The allotrope of carbon is available in crystalline form or amorphous forms. The different allotropes of carbon are diamond, lonsdaleite, graphene, graphite, carbine, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, carbon dots, etc. The zero dimensional carbon allotropes are fullerene, carbon dot, graphene dot, nano diamond. The one dimensional carbon allotropes are single walled carbon nanotubes, multi-walled carbon nanotubes, and carbon nanohorns. The two dimensional carbon allotropes are graphene and multi-layered graphite sheets. The three dimensional carbon allotropes are diamond and graphite [47]. Among available carbon allotropes, graphene is considered as “the mother of all graphitic forms of carbon”, which consists of atoms in a single layer with 2D honeycomb lattice. Graphene has many beneficial properties, including high mobility concerning charge carriers, impermeable to gasses, high thermal conductivity, extremely stiff, high thermal conductivity, high flexible thin films, and others. The several carbon allotropes are considered as a potential candidate in developing biosensors for evaluating food shelf life in terms of food safety and quality monitoring property. Jia et al. (2019) developed a sensor based on label-free fluorescent using graphene to detect aflatoxin B1 molecule in milk samples [48]. The other studies on carbon-allotrope based sensors include the detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ in water and milk samples using graphite oxide based sensor [49] and voltametric sensor for detection of lead, chromium, and zinc from rice on activated carbon fibre rod [50]. Carbon-based nanomaterials are extensively used as biosensors, cancer therapy, drug delivery, cellular imaging, Raman imaging, optical imaging, photodynamic photothermal therapy, cellular therapy, and targeting. Additionally, carbon based sensors are used as gas sensors, biological sensors, mechanical sensors, and humidity sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of nanotechnology in freshness and shelf life detection of food products
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned in the previous section, different organic and inorganic nanomaterials are used for freshness detection and in monitoring the shelf life of food products. In this regard, a detailed discussion for applying the different types of nanomaterials with the applicable selectivity has been discussed in this section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of titanium dioxide based sensors

ce:section.ce:para: The titanium dioxide is used in combination with other materials to develop sensor for shelf life analysis of food products in terms of quality detection and safety detection (Table 2 ). The gas sensor based on Polypyrrole/TiO2 nanocomposite films coated on quartz crystal microbalance substrate is prepared using layer-by-layer self-assembly approach [7]. This sensor is used to evaluate the shelf life of mango, egg, and fish, where the trace irritant gases such as NH3, ethanol, H2S, and trimethylamine can be measured. The sensor has the properties of higher sensitivity and better stability and is sensitive to toxic gases at low concentrations. Further, the silk fibroin fibers are generally fabricated using the degumming method, which involves the removal of sericin protein from the silk cocoons, which can be used with titanium dioxide-polyaniline to develop microsensors. In this context, the microsensor based on titanium dioxide-polyaniline (TiO2-PANI) and silk fibroin fiber (SFF) having a diameter of 12 µm are used in evaluating the freshness of pork [6]. The TiO2-PANI and SFF based microsensor can be developed by in-situ polymerization, where, on the surface of SFF, the composites of TiO2-PANI are deposited. The TiO2-PANI deposited SFF based microsensor provides good sensing activity, whereas, with model gas ammonia, response value and response time of 0.82 and 10 s to NH3 of 100 µg/L was obtained [6]. The microsensor based on TiO2-PANI deposited SFF is used in determining the total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) level in pork to determine the freshness levels. Besides, the use of Schiff base Zn(II) complex/TiO2 as a material is used for developing histamine fluorescence sensor to detect histamine, where the sensor provides a limit of detection of 2.53 × 10−10 M [8]. The sensor is developed by depositing carboxyl substituted Schiff base Zn(II) complex with hydroxypropyl side chain onto TiO2, which is selective for histamine over other amines. Additionally, the gas sensor based on SnO2 thick film with TiO2 (10 wt%) and Pd (1 wt%) is used to detect fish freshness, which provides best sensitivity for trimethylamine (TMA) and selectivity for ammonia and dimethylamine compared to TMA gas [9].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of TiO₂ based sensor to detect synthetic colorants in food
ce:section.ce:para: The two synthetic colorants such as sunset yellow and tartrazine can cause health related issues, including childhood hyperactivity and appearance of asthama [51]. However, compared with natural colorants, synthetic colorants exhibit high stability against oxygen, pH, and light. Further, the synthetic colorants provide low microbial contamination and are also cost effective, which enhances their use in food industries [20]. In this context, the trace colorants such as sunset yellow and tartrazine in food samples can be determined using graphene and mesoporous TiO₂ based electrochemical sensor, which can determine the concentration at low concentrations in a rapid and simultaneous way. The graphene and mesoporous TiO₂ have been used to modify carbon paste electrodes, which can be used to determine the voltammetric behaviors of the mentioned colorants having a detection limit of 8.0 and 6.0 nM for tartrazine and sunset yellow, respectively [51]. The sensor based on graphene and mesoporous TiO₂ is rapid, reliable, and sensitive. Further, the sensor is also used to investigate the synthetic colorants in food products such as ice cream, royal jelly powder, and soft drinks. The electrochemical sensor based on TiO₂-Ag/Polypyrrole nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode can detect tyramine in food samples with a detection limit of 2 × 10−8 M [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of silver nanoparticles based sensors
ce:section.ce:para: It is used in several sectors such as diagnostic applications, cosmetics, human health care, water treatment, drug carrier, textiles, imaging, biosensing, etc. However, as shown in Table 3 , the use of silver nanoparticles for shelf life monitoring and food safety has been mentioned. During meat spoilage, sulphur containing amino acids degrade producing H₂S, which is a volatile gas. Thus, sensor to detect H₂S can act as a meat spoilage indicator for food packaging. In this context, the colorimetric hydrogen sulphide sensor based on gellan gum and AgNPs can be used, where AgNPs have high reactivity to react with H₂S to form Ag₂S [15]. The electrochemical sensor based on AgNPs and graphene oxide nanocomposite on screen printed Ag electrode can detect sodium ions in food samples such as fish sauce and seasoning powder of instant noodles [53]. The electrochemical sensor based on AgNPs and carboxylic short-chain multi-wall carbon nanotubes can be developed by electrodepositing AgNPs on carboxylic short-chain multi-wall carbon nanotubes in the presence of NH3 to detect chloramphenicol [14]. The mentioned electrochemical sensor has several advantages such as easy operation, low cost, high sensitivity, high electrocatalytic activity, and high reproducibility. Carbamazepine is a type of anticonvulsant medication used to treat neuropathic pain and epilepsy.

Additionally, the sensor based on graphene quantum dots and cysteine capped AgNPs is used to detect glyphosate in pulses (peas and lupins) [17]. The L-cysteine modified AgNPs based chemical sensor is selective in detecting Vitamin B1 in food (grapes, peas, tomato) and water samples (sewage, river, and pond) [16]. The electrostatic interactions between positively charged vitamin B1 causes red shift of localized surface plasmon resonance of L-cysteine modified AgNPs from 390 to 580 nm. The various available analytical techniques are used to detect and determine food colorants including thin layer chromatography, spectrophotometric method, high performance liquid chromatography, enzyme linked immunosorbent assay, etc. The techniques are time-consuming and complex. Based on this, the surface enhanced Raman scattering sensor is a rapid technique to determine food colorants. The electrochemical sensor based on Carbamazepine functionalized AgNPs can detect specific toxic dyes such as orange II and rhodamine B [12]. This specific sensor has the detection limit for Rhodamine B, and orange II is 1.2 nM and 1.0 nM, respectively. The sensor has the properties of high sensitivity, economic, and easy fabrication process. The inclusion of graphene increases the Raman spectra of AgNPs. The surface enhanced Raman scattering sensor based on graphene and silver nanocomposite can detect prohibited food colorants, which is a rapid detection method and have a detection limit is as low as 0.1 µM [13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of gold nanoparticles based sensors
ce:section.ce:para:
However, as shown in Table 4 , the use of gold nanoparticles for shelf life monitoring and food safety has been mentioned. The nanoparticles are extensively used for the large surface area, electrical conductivity, chemical properties, biocompatibility, optical properties, and others. Gold nanoparticles have high surface area, making them attractive for biodetection and can be bioconjugated to several ligands, including DNA, antibodies, aptamers, etc. The AuNPs based enhanced chemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor is more sensitive than traditional ELISA, and provides increased sensitivity to immunological assay, where the ECL immunosensor is used for detecting Staphylococcal Enterotoxin B in mushroom, tomato, and meat baby foods with limit of detection ∼ 0.01 ng/mL [22]. Staphylococcal enterotoxins are heat stable toxins that cause food poisoning and many diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis, atopic eczema, and others. Additionally, chemically modified carbon-paste electrode using gold nanoparticles is also used to determine the mentioned food colorants in soft drinks, where the detection limit for tartrazine and sunset yellow are 2.0 × 10−9 mol l−1, and 3.0 × 10−8 mol l−1, respectively [20]. The colorimetric aptasensor based on AuNPs and aptamer is suitable for naked eye detection of Shigella flexneri and the aptasensor is simple, convenient, suitable for on-site detection, highly specific, with a limit of detection is 80 CFU/mL [23]. The mentioned aptasensor is tested for the applicability in smoked salmon samples, which are spiked with different concentrations of Shigella flexneri. The colorimetric sensor based on AuNPs is used in the detection of different reducing sugars such as glucose, fructose, mannose, and lactose, where the redox reaction between the reducing sugars and
AuCl 4 −
occurs producing AuNPs [26]. The several methods for detecting reducing sugars are polarzed light angle measurements, refractometry, potentiometry, amperometry, conductometry, etc. The voltammetric sensor based on AuNPs can be used to determine Sudan I in food samples such as ketchup and chilly sauce. The sensor provides excellent analytical performance, high sensitivity, good reproducibility, good stability, good stability, and good anti-interference ability [24]. Sudan I is a non-ionic fat soluble dye that is used in tomato sauces, chilly sauces, salami, and olive oils to enhance the appearance. The surface imprinted AuNPs based surface enhanced Raman Scattering techniques is used to detect patulin in food products which is specific and sensitive with a limit of detection of 5.37 × 10−12 M [25]. Patulin is a kind of secondary metabolite fungi, neurotoxin, which causes adverse health issues for immunity, fertility, and others. The multiwalled carbon nanotube and gold NPs composites and chitosan based molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor is used for detecting tyramine in yogurt samples [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of various nanoparticles based sensors
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Table 5 , the different types of nanomaterials are used as sensor for shelf life detection and safety analysis of food products. The electrochemical sensor based on zinc oxide nanoflower modified carbon paste electrode is used for the detection of sunset yellow in soft drinks [32]. Additionally, the electrochemical sensor based on zinc oxide nanoflower modified carbon paste electrode has several beneficial properties such as highly sensitive, good selectivity, low cost, and involves simple fabrication method with a detection limit of 0.10 µg/L. The optical fluorescence sensor based on zinc(II) salphen complex can be used in detecting biogenic amine, where it provides good selectivity for histamine and cadaverine with 4.4 × 10−12 M limit of detection for histamine [31]. The thick film sensor based on tin oxide and zinc oxide is used as a vapor sensor that can be used to monitor the quality of potato tubers and dry cured ham during storage [30]. The aluminum doped zinc oxide thin film developed by the sputtering technique is used as a smell sensor for seafood such as Salmon, oyster, squid, sea bream, and sardine, which produces trimethylamine gas during fish degradation [29].

Additionally, the cellulose acetate nanofibers containing alizarin are used as a halochromic sensor for the quality assessment of rainbow trout fish [54]. The edible pH sensor based on immobilized anthocyanin (red cabbage) and bacterial cellulose membrane is used in developing intelligent packaging [55]. Additionally, the nanocomposite based on cellulose and graphene is used as a multifunctional electronic and solvent sensor [56]. The nanofiber bacterial cellulose film is used as an optical sensor for detecting ethylene during banana storage [57]. In this way, different nanomaterials are used in combined form for providing synergistic effect in developing sensors.

Nanosensors are a novel technology, and their application in biological systems is limited. Some nanosensors have the potential to disrupt cell metabolism and homeostasis, altering cellular and molecular profiles and making it difficult to distinguish sensor-induced aberrations from basic biological events. Because of their tiny size and susceptibility to diverse synthesis procedures, nanosensors need a high degree of accuracy to be manufactured, posing extra technological obstacles. Dispersion, establishing repeatable calibration procedures, and using preconcentration and separation processes are all challenges for nanosensors. It is also difficult to integrate the nanosensor with other parts of a sensor package in a reliable manner.

The nanotechnology based sensors are extensively used in shelf life analysis with respect to freshness monitoring, spoilage detection (volatile gas and amines), and safety measures to confirm the safe consumption of food products. The nanomaterials including inorganic nanomaterials, organic nanomaterials, and carbon allotropes, have immense potential in the field of developing sensors for exhibiting numerous properties such as high catalytic activity, chemical property, biological activity, surface chemistry, photocatalytic activity, and others. Additionally, the combined use of the specified nanomaterials also delivers improved sensitivity for several food components with enhanced sensitivity, reproducibility, and selectivity, which are required traits to be used as sensors. Thus, the improved properties availed due to the synergistic effects from the combined use of the nanomaterials making it more attractive for sensor application in shelf life detection of food. Additionally, large scale production of these sensors will be needed in the future with more intense research in this sector to ensure safe, healthy food delivery to end consumers.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Please check the following as appropriate:

All authors have participated in (a) conception and design, or analysis and interpretation of the data; (b) drafting the article or revising it critically for important intellectual content; and (c) approval of the final version. This manuscript has not been submitted to, nor is under review at, another journal or other publishing venue. The authors have no affiliation with any organization with a direct or indirect financial interest in the subject matter discussed in the manuscript. The following authors have affiliations with organizations with direct or indirect financial interest in the subject matter discussed in the manuscript:

Dr. Kshirod Kumar Dash Ghani Khan Choudhury Institute of Engineering and Technology, India
